cataloging_subject.label: The Middle English Metrical Version of the Revelations of Methodius
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous fifteenth-century Middle English verse translation and adaptation preserved uniquely in British Library Stowe MS 953 and printed by Charlotte D'Evelyn in 1918. The record describes the medieval poem, not Pseudo-Methodius's late-seventh-century source work, the unidentified short Latin exemplar, the physical manuscript, D'Evelyn's complete article and Latin appendix, the University of Michigan encoding, or the repository XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: The Middle English Metrical Version of the Revelations of Methodius
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator-adapter
catalog_summary.editor: Charlotte D'Evelyn; transcript prepared by Marguerite Thiebaud
catalog_summary.date: After 1424, probably fifteenth century; uniquely attested in a fifteenth-century manuscript and first printed in 1918
catalog_summary.region: Probably Norfolk, England, on dialectal evidence; the exact place of composition remains unresolved

titles.value: The Middle English Metrical Version of the Revelations of Methodius
notes: Normalized from D'Evelyn's established article title; 'Methodius' is the historical attribution of the source work, not an authorship claim for the Middle English poem.

titles.value: The Middle-English Metrical Version of the Revelations of Methodius; With a Study of the Influence of Methodius in Middle-English Writings

scope.description: Title of the 1918 article that first prints the poem

notes: The second clause names D'Evelyn's surrounding study rather than the medieval work itself.
Internal section V heading on printed p. 156; no manuscript title is asserted.

titles.value: Sum tyme was there a man of state
with grace he glad vs with all goode. Amen.

agents.name: Marguerite Thiebaud
notes: Bryn Mawr graduate scholar in English, 1912-1914, who died in March 1914; D'Evelyn credits her with the working transcript, source identification, and initial Cursor Mundi comparisons.
The poem translates and paraphrases the abbreviated Latin Revelationes tradition; no exact physical Latin exemplar or personal name is known.
D'Evelyn demonstrates substantive supplementary use of Cursor Mundi and narrower verbal and narrative borrowing from Andrew of Wyntoun's Original Chronicle.

scope.description: First printed scholarly presentation, notes, and source study
Working transcript made from rotographs of Stowe MS 953

date_statements.display: After 1424 and probably within the fifteenth century
notes: D'Evelyn's proposed use of Wyntoun supplies the post-1424 terminus; the current British Library record dates the sole witness only broadly to the fifteenth century. This range is not a date copied from ownership evidence.

date_statements.display: Uniquely attested in fifteenth-century British Library Stowe MS 953
notes: The date belongs to the witness and only bounds the poem's production; it is not treated as an exact composition date.

date_statements.display: First printed by Charlotte D'Evelyn in PMLA 33.2 (1918), pp. 156-182
notes: This is the first verified printed edition found in the reviewed sources, not the medieval translation date.

D'Evelyn argues for a Norfolk writer from linguistic features and discusses early Norfolk ownership. The linguistic proposal is retained as probable; ownership at Toft Monks and by Francis Blomefield is not independently converted into a composition place.

language_statements.label: English

scope.description: D'Evelyn's heading footnote and thirty textual notes retained in the encoding

notes: Editorial English does not make the medieval work substantively bilingual.

form_statements.description: A 975-line stanzaic metrical translation; brief editorial prose notes are apparatus rather than a substantive prose component of the work

subject_statements.label: Creation, cosmology, and the natural world
Death, salvation, and the Last Judgment
Anti-Muslim polemic and religious violence

scope.description: The hostile apocalyptic representation of Ishmaelites/Saracens and their conflict with Christians

notes: This subject heading describes the work's polemical content; it does not endorse its representation.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English metrical adaptation of the abbreviated Latin Revelationes of Pseudo-Methodius recounts world history from Creation, Adam and Eve, and the patriarchs through kingdoms and wars, then turns to prophecy: hostile depictions of the sons of Ishmael or Saracens, Christian defeat and recovery, Gog and Magog, Antichrist, Enoch and Elijah, and the Last Judgment. D'Evelyn argues that the translator supplemented the Latin source substantially from Cursor Mundi and in a smaller cluster of passages from Andrew of Wyntoun's Original Chronicle.

CME00069 encodes only D'Evelyn's internal section V, the 975-line Middle English poem on printed pp. 156-182, with its heading footnote and thirty textual notes. It omits the article's source study on pp. 135-156, notes after the poem on pp. 182-191, and short Latin-text appendix on pp. 192-203.

The sole witness is a small paper manuscript of sixteen medieval folios. D'Evelyn's printed foliation places the poem on fols. 4r-19r; ownership material occupies f. 19v and front matter includes a bookplate and later provenance notes. The current British Library catalog supplies only a total extent of sixteen folios, so the historical-to-current pencil-foliation crosswalk still requires image or repository verification.

extent.description: The XML contains one poem division with 975 direct L elements in 123 LG groups, distributed as 116 eight-line, five seven-line, and two six-line groups. It also retains 27 printed-page breaks (pp. 156-182), 30 explicit folio-side milestones from f. 4v through f. 19r, 30 editorial notes, and an Amen closer. D'Evelyn states that the manuscript marks no stanza divisions, so LG totals describe the edition/encoding representation rather than manuscript layout.

content_parts.label: The Middle English Metrical Version of the Revelations of Methodius
content_parts.summary: The complete 975-line verse translation and adaptation represented by CME00069, from its Methodius prologue to its final prayer and Amen.

locators.value: fols. 4r-19r; f. 19v carries ownership material outside the poem

notes: The current repository's pencil foliation has not been imaged or crosswalked. The fols. 4r-19r locator is therefore explicitly tied to D'Evelyn's 1918 notation rather than silently presented as a verified current digital label.

related_works.title: Latin Recension 2 of the Revelationes of Pseudo-Methodius
notes: D'Evelyn calls this the short Latin text; Twomey identifies the abbreviated Latin form transmitted in England as Recension 2. The translator's exact physical exemplar is unknown.

related_works.title: Cursor Mundi
notes: Supplementary source for creation, astronomy, Antichrist, Enoch and Elijah, and numerous verbal or rhyme correspondences; it does not replace the Latin Revelationes as the poem's principal source.

related_works.title: Andrew of Wyntoun's Original Chronicle
notes: D'Evelyn proposes a narrow cluster of narrative and verbal borrowings in lines 149-178; this relationship supplies her post-1424 terminus.

evidence.citation: General Corpus CME00069 source XML and structural inspection
evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD/REVDESCR; IDG; TEXT/@LANG; BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem']; HEAD, PB, LG, L, MILESTONE, NOTE, and CLOSER
evidence.summary: Verifies all three CME00069 identifiers, the 2016 revision description, exact encoded boundary, Middle English language declaration, 975 verse lines in 123 line groups, 27 page breaks, thirty folio milestones, thirty notes, incipit, explicit, and repository checksum.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library CME00069 current raw XML and Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse collection page
evidence.summary: The current raw XML identifies U-M, the 2018 header production, D'Evelyn's PMLA source, Middle English language, 27-page extent, and a public-domain/no-known-restrictions statement. Canonical comparison confirms that its EEBO body is byte-equivalent after XML canonicalization to the pinned repository artifact even though the current file adds a header, stylesheet, and commented revision wrapper.

evidence.citation: Charlotte D'Evelyn, 'The Middle-English Metrical Version of the Revelations of Methodius; With a Study of the Influence of Methodius in Middle-English Writings,' PMLA 33.2 (1918), 135-203
evidence.locator: article pp. 135-203; manuscript and source study pp. 151-156; poem pp. 156-182; notes pp. 182-191; Latin appendix pp. 192-203
evidence.summary: Direct scan inspection verifies title, author, volume/issue/pages, Marguerite Thiebaud's rotograph-derived transcript, comparison with the original manuscript, the unique Stowe witness, proposed Norfolk dialect, principal short Latin source, supplementary Cursor Mundi and Wyntoun use, post-1424 terminus, verse form, exact poem boundary, foliation, and editorial treatment.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Stowe MS 953
evidence.summary: Confirms the exact current shelfmark, incipit, contents through Antichrist and Last Judgment, rough octosyllabic quatrains, red rhyme indications, paper support, sixteen-folio extent, fifteenth-century date, English language, early and later ownership, current onsite route, and absence of an item-specific Digitised Content link.

evidence.citation: British Museum, Catalogue of the Stowe Manuscripts in the British Museum, volume 1 (London: Trustees, 1895), 637
evidence.locator: printed p. 637, Internet Archive n656, PDF p. 657
evidence.summary: The complete catalog scan preserves the historical Stowe 953 entry describing the poem, its sixteen-folio paper witness, red rhyme indications, fifteenth-century date, and provenance; the current BL record closely carries this description forward.

evidence.citation: Michael W. Twomey, 'The Revelationes of Pseudo-Methodius and Scriptural Study at Salisbury in the Eleventh Century,' in Source of Wisdom, ed. Charles D. Wright, Frederick M. Biggs, and Thomas N. Hall (Toronto: University of Toronto Press, 2007), 370-386
evidence.summary: Provides modern reception-history context: Pseudo-Methodius was composed in Syriac about 690, translated into Greek and then Latin, and the Latin and Middle English versions transmitted in England normally represent abbreviated Recension 2. It supports source-work identity without identifying the Middle English translator's physical exemplar.

evidence.citation: Middle English Dictionary quotation record for Methodius(3), Stowe 953
evidence.summary: Confirms the MED short title Methodius(3), shelfmark abbreviation Stw 953, a1500 dating convention, and the poem's incipit.

Provides the material and digital source chain, work-to-source relations, exact cached-object mappings, access routes, rights evidence, editorial practices, and unresolved questions used by this metadata record.

open_questions.question: How does D'Evelyn's 1918 foliation of Stowe MS 953 (poem fols. 4r-19r; ownership on f. 19v) correspond to the manuscript's current pencil foliation and complete physical sequence?
next_steps: Request a complete color photographic object from British Library Imaging Services, specifying covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, all front and rear flyleaves, blanks, every manuscript leaf and both sides, f. 19v ownership material, and edges where offered; ask the Library to identify current pencil foliation before mapping the poem separately.

open_questions.question: Do Marguerite Thiebaud's transcript, notes, or source rotographs survive, and who performed the final comparison with Stowe MS 953?
next_steps: Trace Bryn Mawr English Department records, Thiebaud papers, D'Evelyn papers and correspondence, PMLA editorial records, and British Museum reproduction files; retain the passive comparison statement without assigning an unsupported collator.

open_questions.question: Can modern linguistic and textual analysis refine or revise D'Evelyn's post-1424 Wyntoun dependency and probable Norfolk localization?
next_steps: Compare the complete manuscript against current LALME data and modern editions of Cursor Mundi, Wyntoun, and Latin Revelationes Recension 2; keep provenance, dialect, source relation, and composition place as separate claims.

notes: Pseudo-Methodius is the pseudonymous source-author attribution, not the author of this anonymous Middle English translation.
Norfolk ownership and Francis Blomefield's bookplate are provenance facts. They support context but are not, by themselves, a composition-place argument.
D'Evelyn's 123 printed/encoded stanza groups are editorial structures because the manuscript marks no stanza divisions.
The hostile representation of Ishmaelites and Saracens is described explicitly as anti-Muslim polemic so discovery metadata does not neutralize or endorse the text's rhetoric.
